macro_segments.title: Introduction to Reishit HaGez and the Mishnah's Basic Parameters

micro_segments.title: Opening: Reishit HaGez as the first gift from non-korbanot animals
Mishnah: Applicability in Eretz Yisrael and outside, with or without the Beit HaMikdash
Stringencies of Zroa Lechayayim veKeivah compared to Reishit HaGez

macro_segments.title: Definition of Merubeh (Plurality) and the Minimum Number of Sheep

micro_segments.title: Etymology of Tzon (flock) and why shearing applies only to sheep, not goats or cattle
The need for merubeh (plurality): analysis of the word tzon as requiring a flock
Beit Shammai vs. Beit Hillel on minimum number: two sheep versus five sheep
Rabbi Dosa ben Harkinas: requiring minimum wool per sheep for obligation
Chachamim: five sheep with any amount (kol shehu) suffices

macro_segments.title: The Amount to Be Given to the Kohen and the Status of Clean versus Dyed Wool

micro_segments.title: Amount required: five sela'im in Judea, ten in Galilee
The wool must be laundered (meluban) and not dirty
Discussion of wool cleaning and lanolin: practical considerations
Minimum requirement: enough to make a small garment
Case: seller did not give wool to kohen before dyeing it—exemption
Dyeing as transformation and theft: melacha that destroys ownership of matnot kehunah
Clean wool without dyeing obligates giving to kohen

macro_segments.title: Ownership Requirements: Non-Jew's Wool and Resale of Shearings

micro_segments.title: Buying wool from a non-Jew's sheep: exemption
Buying wool from another Jew's shearings: seller must give from remainder
If seller retains no wool: buyer becomes obligated
Different types of wool: black and white, or male and female
Different categories as separate shearings: each party gives from their own type

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Why Sanctified Animals Are Excluded from Reishit HaGez

micro_segments.title: Question: Why does the obligation not apply to qedoshim (sanctified animals)?
Answer: The verse צאנך (your sheep) excludes hekdesh ownership
Why a verse was needed: without it, one might think qedoshim are obligated
Distinction: kodshei mizbe'ach (sacrifices) versus kodshei bedek habayis (temple treasury)
Rabbi Eliezer: kodshei bedek habayis are forbidden to be shorn (rabbinic restriction)

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Scenarios Where One Might Think Obligation Applies to Sanctified Animals

micro_segments.title: First scenario: redemption and giving to kohen as a solution if shearer sheared sanctified wool
Problem: wool cannot be redeemed because it cannot be stood up (alive)
Resolution via Rav Mani bar Patish: sanctifying animal except for wool
Even if you own the wool from a sanctified sheep, you don't give to kohen

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Why Sacrifices Cannot Have Partial Sanctity Retained

micro_segments.title: Problem with sacrifices: can't subdivide sanctity by retaining wool ownership
Shearing weakens the animal, impinging on sacrifice's sanctity (me'ilah issue)
Proof from Temurah: sacrificial sanctity defines the whole animal, not divisible parts

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Implications for Terumah, Challah, and Peah—When Singular Possessive Does Not Demand Sole Ownership

micro_segments.title: Challah: reishit (first) might exclude partnership via analogy to reishit hagez
But plural arisoseichem (your troughs) includes partnership in challah
Alternative: arisoseichem teaches the amount (based on wilderness omer)
Peah: sadcha (singular field) would exclude partnership, but plural harvest overrides
Sadcha still excludes partnership with non-Jew

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Why Sacrifices Cannot Have Partial Sanctity Retained (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Yose debate: sanctity of a part that lacks soul versus has soul
Essence of sacrifice: once a part is sanctified, the whole animal is defined as korban

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Implications for Terumah, Challah, and Peah—When Singular Possessive Does Not Demand Sole Ownership (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rava introduces principle: Rabbi Elai concedes partnership obligates for terumah
Verse trumoteichem (your plural) overrides singular possessive degancha
Degancha (singular) still excludes partnership with non-Jew

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Rava's Case of Sanctifying the Wool Itself

micro_segments.title: Alternative scenario: sanctifying only the wool, not the sheep
Question: must one redeem the wool and then give to kohen?
Answer from תתן (give): excludes cases requiring extra step of redemption

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Partnership with Non-Jews and Partnership with Jews

micro_segments.title: Question: does צאנך (your sheep) exclude partnership with non-Jew, with Jew, or both?
Rabbi Elai's view: צאנך excludes even partnership with a Jew (singular possessive)
Chachamim's view: צאנך only excludes non-Jewish partnership
Source for Chachamim: word reishit appears twice to separate categories
Rabbi Elai's counter: connection via vav (and) links exclusions from terumah

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Baraita on Field Ownership by Jew and Non-Jew—Yesh Breira versus Ein Breira

micro_segments.title: Setup: Jew and non-Jew own field in partnership; harvest and divide
Yesh breira (Rabbi): grain retroactively assigned to original ownership portions
Ein breira (Rabban Gamliel): division is clear; all Jew's portion obligated
Key point: all agree non-Jew's portion is exempt, Jew's portion obligated
Rashi's resolution for mixed ownership: separate other grain for terumah/maaser
Difference from animals: grain is divisible; animals are indivisible units
